plainspoken.description: Short sentences, common words, no jargon unless the audience actually uses it. If a ten-dollar word exists where a two-dollar word works, use the two-dollar word.
rules: Use short sentences.
Use common, everyday words.
Use technical jargon only when the target audience genuinely uses it (e.g., fill power, denier, waterproof rating).
Never use a complex word when a simpler one works.

confident.description: Yowie knows what it built and why. No hedging, no qualifiers. State what the product does. If there is a trade-off, state that too.
rules: State product capabilities directly without hedging.
Do not use phrases like 'we believe' or 'we strive'.
Acknowledge trade-offs honestly rather than hiding or softening them.
Never use qualifiers that undermine a direct claim.

restrained.description: No exclamation points. No superlatives without evidence. Undersell and overdeliver. 'Built for' is a phrase Yowie uses often.
rules: Never use exclamation points.
Never use superlatives ('best', 'ultimate', 'perfect') without concrete evidence.
Prefer 'built for' over aspirational or hyperbolic framing.
Let the product facts do the selling — avoid hype language.

dry.description: A sense of humor that doesn't try hard. The humor lives in product names and the occasional aside, not in the copy itself.
rules: Humor belongs in product names and brief asides, not in marketing copy.
Never force jokes or try-hard wit.
Deadpan tone is appropriate — let the reader find the funny.
Avoid playful or whimsical language in technical or feature descriptions.
Lead with specs and materials.
Use fragment sentences where appropriate for density.
Include precise measurements and ratings.
No lifestyle framing or aspirational language.
product_page.example: 500D Cordura body. Rated to 45 lbs. 4 lbs 2 oz.

rules: Keep it short — respect the reader's time.
Introduce the product, name the key benefit, point to details.
No preamble or throat-clearing.
Conversational but not casual to the point of vagueness.
email.example: New tent. Low profile. Handles wind. Details below.

rules: Lean into dry humor and understatement.
One idea per post — no stacking of features.
Let the product name or a single fact carry the post.
Never use hashtag-heavy or influencer-style copy.
social_media.example: The Dirtbag. Named after its target market.

rules: Acknowledge the issue immediately.
State the resolution clearly and without hedging.
No corporate filler phrases or apologetic over-explanation.
Move directly to next steps.
customer_service.example: That shouldn't happen. Send a photo and we'll replace it.

backcountry.approach: Write with assumed technical literacy. Provide full specs and material details without translation. Trust that the reader knows what the data means.
assumed_knowledge: The difference between comfort rating and lower limit
What DAC poles are and why they matter
guidelines: Include precise technical specs without simplifying them.
Do not explain what specs mean unless explaining a genuine trade-off.
Use terms like fill power, denier, DWR, baffle construction freely.
Speak peer-to-peer — the reader is as experienced as the copywriter.

casual.approach: Translate specs into outcomes. Focus on what the product does, not how it works. Avoid gear-nerd jargon. Still plainspoken, confident, restrained, and dry — just with plain-English framing.
assumed_knowledge: General awareness of product categories (coolers, tents, bags)
Experience with cheaper versions of the same product failing
Price sensitivity and skepticism of premium brand markups
guidelines: Translate technical specs into observable outcomes (e.g., 'keeps ice five days' not '3-inch pressure-injected insulation').
Focus on what the product does rather than how it is built.
Avoid jargon specific to gear enthusiasts.
Connect quality to value — frame durability as a savings argument.
The 'no brand tax' message resonates strongly with this audience.

approved.note: Core Yowie verb — implies intentional construction and purpose.
Use when emphasizing design precision or structural choices.
Use when describing intentional form or feature decisions.
Use with specific numeric values — rated to 45 lbs, comfort-rated to 30°F.
Use only when actual testing occurred; pairs with measurable outcomes.
Use with quantified results — weight, capacity, retention times.
Preferred over 'tough' or 'rugged' — implies longevity without bravado.
Use for performance consistency across conditions.
Use when contrasting with aesthetic-only features or brand-driven design.
Action verb for describing product performance — 'handles wind', 'handles loads'.
Use for control and regulation — 'manages load transfer', 'manages moisture'.
Use for outcome-focused descriptions — 'performs in conditions'.
Use to describe Yowie's communication approach and trade-off transparency.
Use to describe simplicity in design or communication.
Use to describe Yowie's voice and product intent.

forbidden.reason: Hyperbolic — implies a break from prior art that rarely exists.
Marketing cliché with no specific meaning.
Vague and overused — describe the specific innovation instead.
Performative emotion — Yowie demonstrates commitment through product, not feeling.
Vague and aspirational — not Yowie's register.
Lifestyle brand language — implies editorial taste over function.
Aspirational lifestyle framing — Yowie describes conditions, not emotions.
Aspirational framing — too narrative and lifestyle-coded.
Hyperbolic enthusiasm — antithetical to restrained, dry voice.
Superlative without evidence — Yowie never claims best, it describes what the product does.
Superlative without evidence — implies nothing better exists.
Superlative without evidence — also implies no trade-offs, which contradicts honest voice.

conditional.rule: Do not use as a leading marketing claim. Use only as a factual description when stating specific material or packaging choices.
conditional.incorrect: Yowie is committed to sustainable gear.

conditional.rule: Avoid as a standalone descriptor. If used, pair with a specific, verifiable claim about materials or packaging.
conditional.correct: Recycled polyester fly fabric and minimal packaging — no clamshells, no tissue paper.
conditional.incorrect: An eco-friendly tent for environmentally conscious campers.

conditional.rule: Use only when weight is a genuine design goal for the product. Do not apply to products where weight is intentionally secondary to durability.
conditional.correct: Ridgeline 22L. 1 lb 6 oz. Built light for summit pushes.

built_for_job.headline: Built for the job, not the brand.
built_for_job.description: Every product is designed around its specific use case. No features for the spec sheet. No materials for the marketing copy.

resonance.backcountry: We respect the job you're doing and built gear that matches it.
resonance.casual: This does what you need. Nothing extra.

quality_you_verify.headline: Quality you verify, not quality we claim.
quality_you_verify.description: Real specs, real materials data, real-world performance. The products hold up to scrutiny because they're built to hold up to scrutiny.

resonance.backcountry: Dig into the specs. The more you research, the more you'll find to like.
resonance.casual: Check the reviews. Compare the build. We're not hiding anything.

pay_for_gear.headline: Pay for the gear, not the logo.
pay_for_gear.description: Minimal branding. No aspirational marketing. No sponsorship deals. The savings get reinvested into materials and construction.

resonance.backcountry: We respect your intelligence — you're paying for materials, not marketing.
resonance.casual: Same quality as the big names. No brand tax.

responsible_materials.headline: Responsible materials, zero grandstanding.
responsible_materials.description: Recycled fabrics, natural insulation, minimal packaging — because these choices make sense, not because they make good Instagram posts.

resonance.backcountry: Good materials happen to be responsible materials. That's the whole story.
resonance.casual: We use recycled and natural materials where it makes sense. No lecture.

basecamp_45l.name: Basecamp 45L
basecamp_45l.description: Multi-day backcountry hauler built for loads up to 45 pounds over rough, trailless terrain.

ridgeline_22l.name: Ridgeline 22L
ridgeline_22l.description: Stripped-down daypack for summit pushes, day hikes, and fast-and-light side trips.

lowline_2p.name: Lowline 2P
lowline_2p.description: Two-person, three-season tent built low and tight for wind resistance.

basecamp_4p.name: Basecamp 4P
basecamp_4p.description: Four-person basecamp tent for multi-day trips in variable conditions.

dirtbag_30f.name: Dirtbag 30°F
dirtbag_30f.description: Three-season bag for campers who sleep out from spring through fall.

burrow_0f.name: Burrow 0°F
burrow_0f.description: Winter bag for cold-weather specialists. Comfort-rated to 0°F.

slab_35qt.name: Slab 35QT
slab_35qt.description: 35-quart hard cooler that keeps ice for days, not hours.

foldout.name: Foldout Soft Cooler
foldout.description: Collapsible soft cooler for day trips, grocery runs, and anywhere a hard cooler is overkill.

backcountry_hikers.name: Backcountry Hikers
backcountry_hikers.profile: Experienced hikers with 5+ years backcountry travel. Distrust marketing-heavy brands. Research extensively. Loyalty goes to products, not companies.

thru_hikers.name: Thru-Hikers
thru_hikers.profile: Weight-conscious but not weight-obsessed. Need gear that performs over 2,000+ miles. Community-driven purchase decisions.

casual_car_camping.name: Casual / Car Camping
casual_car_camping.profile: Quality-aware but not gear-obsessed. Tired of replacing cheap gear. See premium brands as overpriced. Shorter research cycles, more influenced by recommendations.

cold_weather.name: Cold-Weather Specialists
cold_weather.profile: Conditions-driven buyers. Most spec-literate segment. Trust independent lab testing. Will pay premium if specs justify it.
